response: To solve this problem we must select three distinct rows and three distinct columns from the available six options since no two rooks can share a row or column. The number of ways to choose three rows from six is calculated as the combination of six items taken three at a time which equals twenty. Similarly the number of ways to choose three columns from six is also twenty. Once the rows and columns are selected we must place the three indistinguishable rooks at the intersections of these chosen lines. For any specific set of three rows and three columns there are exactly two ways to arrange the rooks so that no two share a row or column because the rooks are indistinguishable. We multiply the number of row choices by the number of column choices and then by the number of valid arrangements for each set. This gives us twenty times twenty times two which equals eight hundred. Therefore there are eight hundred ways to arrange the rooks on the board under the given conditions.
